section.title: Wanderings in the Wilderness
section.sectionRationale: Moses continues the historical review, tracing Israel's long detour around Edom, Moab, and Ammon before the first great victory over Sihon, showing that the same LORD who governs Israel's inheritance also governs the boundaries of every nation.
section.thesis: As Israel's forty years of wandering draw to a close, Moses recounts how the LORD scrupulously honored the God-given lands of Edom, Moab, and Ammon, and then hardened Sihon's heart to bring about the first great victory that would embolden Israel for the conquest ahead.
section.frameNote: Five movements: passing by Edom in peace; passing by Moab, with a note on the peoples God displaced before them; passing by Ammon, with a similar note; the command to engage Sihon; and Sihon's refusal, defeat, and total destruction.

verses.text: Then we turned back and headed for the wilderness by way of the Red Sea, as the LORD had instructed me, and for many days we wandered around Mount Seir.
At this time the LORD said to me,
“You have been wandering around this hill country long enough; turn to the north
and command the people: ‘You will pass through the territory of your brothers, the descendants of Esau, who live in Seir. They will be afraid of you, so you must be very careful.
Do not provoke them, for I will not give you any of their land, not even a footprint, because I have given Mount Seir to Esau as his {{yerushah|possession}}.
You are to pay them in silver for the food you eat and the water you drink.’”

Indeed, the LORD your God has blessed you in all the work of your hands. He has watched over your journey through this vast wilderness. The LORD your God has been with you these forty years, and you have lacked nothing.

So we passed by our brothers, the descendants of Esau, who live in Seir. We turned away from the Arabah road, which comes up from Elath and Ezion-geber, and traveled along the road of the Wilderness of Moab.

units.label: Passing by Moab
units.note: the LORD's providence over every nation

verses.text: Then the LORD said to me, “Do not harass the Moabites or provoke them to war, for I will not give you any of their land, because I have given Ar to the descendants of Lot as their possession.”
(The Emites used to live there, a people great and many, as tall as the Anakites.
Like the Anakites, they were also regarded as Rephaim, though the Moabites called them Emites.

The Horites used to live in Seir, but the descendants of Esau drove them out. They destroyed the Horites from before them and settled in their place, just as Israel did in the land that the LORD gave them as their possession.)
“Now arise and cross over the Brook of Zered.” So we crossed over the Brook of Zered.

The time we spent traveling from Kadesh-barnea until we crossed over the Brook of Zered was thirty-eight years, until that entire generation of fighting men had perished from the camp, as the LORD had sworn to them.
Indeed, the LORD's hand was against them, to eliminate them from the camp, until they had all perished.
Now when all the fighting men among the people had died,
the LORD said to me,
“Today you are going to cross the border of Moab at Ar.
But when you get close to the Ammonites, do not harass them or provoke them, for I will not give you any of the land of the Ammonites. I have given it to the descendants of Lot as their possession.”
(That too was regarded as the land of the Rephaim, who used to live there, though the Ammonites called them Zamzummites.
They were a people great and many, as tall as the Anakites. But the LORD destroyed them from before the Ammonites, who drove them out and settled in their place,
just as He had done for the descendants of Esau who lived in Seir, when He destroyed the Horites from before them. They drove them out and have lived in their place to this day.
And the Avvim, who lived in villages as far as Gaza, were destroyed by the Caphtorites, who came out of Caphtor and settled in their place.)

units.label: The command to engage Sihon
units.note: I have delivered him into your hand

verses.text: “Arise, set out, and cross the Arnon Valley. See, I have delivered into your hand Sihon the Amorite, king of Heshbon, and his land. Begin to take possession of it and engage him in battle.
This very day I will begin to put the dread and fear of you upon all the nations under heaven. They will hear the reports of you and tremble in anguish because of you.”
So from the Wilderness of Kedemoth I sent messengers with an offer of peace to Sihon king of Heshbon, saying,
“Let us pass through your land; we will stay on the main road. We will not turn to the right or to the left.
You can sell us food to eat and water to drink in exchange for silver. Only let us pass through on foot,
just as the descendants of Esau who live in Seir and the Moabites who live in Ar did for us, until we cross the Jordan into the land that the LORD our God is giving us.”

But Sihon king of Heshbon would not let us pass through, for the LORD your God had made his spirit {{qashah|stubborn}} and his heart obstinate, that He might deliver him into your hand, as is the case this day.
Then the LORD said to me, “See, I have begun to deliver Sihon and his land over to you. Now begin to conquer and possess his land.”
So Sihon and his whole army came out for battle against us at Jahaz.
And the LORD our God delivered him over to us, and we defeated him and his sons and his whole army.
At that time we captured all his cities and devoted to destruction the people of every city, including women and children. We left no survivors.
We carried off for ourselves only the livestock and the plunder from the cities we captured.
From Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Valley, along with the city in the valley, even as far as Gilead, not one city had walls too high for us. The LORD our God gave us all of them.
But you did not go near the land of the Ammonites, or the land along the banks of the Jabbok River, or the cities of the hill country, or any place that the LORD our God had forbidden.

yerushah.gloss: Possession, inheritance. The same word used for Israel's inheritance of Canaan is applied here to Esau's, Moab's, and Ammon's lands, showing that the LORD who apportions Israel's inheritance also apportions the boundaries of every nation, Acts 17:26.

qashah.gloss: To be hard, stubborn, obstinate. Used of Sihon's spirit here just as Pharaoh's heart is hardened in Exodus, describing God's sovereign hand working through, not against, genuine human stubbornness to bring about His purposes.

basic.context: Moses continues retelling the wilderness years: Israel's long, obedient detour around the lands of Edom, Moab, and Ammon, God's kinsmen through Esau and Lot, followed by the command to engage and defeat Sihon, the first Amorite king blocking their path to the land.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative retold to show two things at once: God's fairness toward nations outside Israel's covenant, and His sovereign hand in bringing about Israel's first victory. It is descriptive of a specific redemptive-historical moment, not a manual for handling every neighboring nation today.
basic.originalLanguages: The word for Esau's, Moab's, and Ammon's lands is yerushah, possession or inheritance, the same word used for Israel's own inheritance of Canaan, underlining that the LORD governs every nation's boundaries. Sihon's refusal comes because the LORD made his spirit qashah, stubborn, echoing the hardening of Pharaoh's heart in Exodus.
basic.typology: The pattern of God hardening a hostile ruler's heart to bring about a greater deliverance recalls Pharaoh and anticipates how God works even through human resistance and evil intentions to accomplish His redemptive purposes, climactically at the cross, Acts 2:23.
basic.christ: Sihon's total defeat becomes the first fulfillment of the LORD's promise to fight for Israel, a pattern that finds its ultimate expression in Christ's victory over every hostile power, Colossians 2:15, where even the wrath of His enemies serves to accomplish God's saving plan.
basic.god: God is scrupulously just, honoring the land grants He made to Edom, Moab, and Ammon even though they are outside His covenant with Israel; He is also sovereign over hardened hearts, using even Sihon's stubbornness to advance His purposes for His people.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 4:21 and 7:3 describe the LORD hardening Pharaoh's heart in a similar way. Acts 17:26 says God determined the boundaries of every nation. Numbers 21:21-30 gives the parallel account of Sihon's defeat.
basic.oneStory: This chapter shows the God of Israel as Lord over every nation's borders and every ruler's heart, whether friendly like Edom or hostile like Sihon. The same sovereign hand that measured out the nations' lands now begins to clear the way for Israel's inheritance, foreshadowing the greater victory Christ wins over every hostile power.

context.literary: This section forms the middle of Moses' historical review, a travelogue moving south to north around Edom, Moab, and Ammon before turning to the first military victory of the conquest narrative, over Sihon king of Heshbon.
context.historical: The thirty-eight years mentioned in verse 14 mark the long consequence of the unbelief described in chapter one; this generation has waited out a full sentence of judgment before God begins to act decisively on their behalf.
context.canonical: The pattern of God allotting land to specific peoples, and later hardening a ruler's heart to accomplish His purposes, echoes the exodus narrative and anticipates the New Testament's teaching that all authority and every nation's boundaries rest under God's sovereign hand.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Israel that the LORD who is bringing them into their inheritance is the same LORD who justly ordered the inheritances of other nations, and that their coming victories rest entirely on His sovereign initiative, not their own strength.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a historical account of a specific stage in Israel's conquest, describing God's dealings with particular nations at a particular moment in redemptive history. It does not prescribe a pattern for how modern nations should treat their neighbors; its abiding truth is God's sovereignty over all peoples and rulers.

debates.question: Does God's hardening of Sihon's heart (v.30) remove Sihon's own responsibility for refusing peace?

views.label: God's sovereign hardening works through, not against, real human choice
views.case: Scripture consistently holds both truths together, as with Pharaoh: God hardens, yet the ruler is described as making his own obstinate choice, and is held responsible for it.

views.label: The hardening should be read primarily as God's righteous judgment on prior wickedness
views.case: Some read this hardening as God confirming Sihon in a hostility he had already chosen, similar to Romans 1's language of God giving people over to what they already desired.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm that Sihon is genuinely responsible for refusing peace, while also affirming that nothing in this outcome falls outside God's sovereign purpose for Israel's good.

study.originalLanguages: Yerushah, possession or inheritance, is applied to Edom's, Moab's, and Ammon's lands exactly as it will be applied to Israel's, showing a single sovereign hand ordering every nation's borders. Sihon's heart is made qashah, stubborn, the same pattern of hardening seen with Pharaoh, so that his resistance becomes the occasion for Israel's first great victory.

typology.type: God hardening Sihon's heart, echoing Pharaoh
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates how God sovereignly works even through hostile human resistance to accomplish His redemptive purposes, supremely at the cross, Acts 2:23, 4:27-28.

typology.type: The devoted destruction of Sihon's kingdom
typology.fulfillment: A first act of the LORD fighting for His people, pointing toward Christ's total, final victory over every hostile power, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: The defeat of Sihon is the first great victory of the conquest, and it comes entirely from the LORD's initiative, not Israel's strength, since even Sihon's stubborn refusal serves God's purpose. This pattern, hostile powers unwittingly serving God's saving plan, reaches its fullest expression at the cross, where the very rulers who opposed Christ accomplished exactly what God had planned beforehand, and finds its consummation in Christ's total victory over every power, Colossians 2:15.
study.god: God is meticulously fair, honoring the inheritances He gave to Edom, Moab, and Ammon even though Israel could easily have overpowered them; and He is sovereign over the hearts of hostile kings, turning Sihon's stubbornness into the occasion for Israel's deliverance, exactly as He did with Pharaoh.

crossReferences.note: The LORD hardens Pharaoh's heart in the same pattern seen here with Sihon.
God determined the appointed times and boundaries of every nation, the same truth this chapter illustrates for Edom, Moab, and Ammon.
The parallel narrative account of Israel's defeat of Sihon.
Christ's total victory over every hostile power, of which Sihon's defeat is an early foreshadowing.

study.oneStory: The God who justly measured out land for Edom, Moab, and Ammon is the same God who now clears the way for Israel's inheritance, even turning a hostile king's stubbornness to serve His purposes. This sovereignty over nations and hearts anticipates the greater victory of Christ, who triumphs over every hostile power for the sake of His people.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD tell Israel not to provoke Edom, Moab, or Ammon (vv.5, 9, 19)?

options.text: Because He had already given each of those lands to their respective descendants as a possession.
options.note: All three passages give this exact reason: God had already granted these lands to Esau's and Lot's descendants.

options.text: Because Israel was too weak to fight them.
options.note: The text gives no indication of weakness; the reason given is God's prior land grant to these peoples.

options.text: Because Israel had signed treaties with them.
options.note: No treaty is mentioned; the restriction comes directly from the LORD's command.

options.text: Because those nations posed no threat at all.
options.note: Verse 4 notes they were afraid of Israel, showing Israel could have threatened them; the restraint was commanded, not circumstantial.

quiz.q: What happened to the earlier inhabitants of the lands later held by Moab, Ammon, and Edom (vv.10-12, 20-23)?

options.text: They too were displaced by the LORD's providence, just as Israel would displace the Canaanites.
options.note: The parenthetical notes describe the Emites, Zamzummites, Horites, and Avvim all being destroyed or displaced before these nations settled the land.

options.text: They were peacefully absorbed into these nations.
options.note: The text describes destruction and displacement, not peaceful absorption.

options.text: They still lived there at the time of Moses' address.
options.note: Verse 22 specifically notes the earlier peoples were destroyed 'to this day', meaning they were long gone by Moses' time.

options.text: The text says nothing happened to them.
options.note: Verses 10-12, 20-23 describe their fate in detail.

quiz.q: What reason does verse 30 give for Sihon's refusal to let Israel pass through peacefully?

options.text: The LORD had made his spirit stubborn and his heart obstinate so He might deliver him into Israel's hand.
options.note: This is exactly what verse 30 states, echoing the hardening of Pharaoh's heart.

options.text: Sihon simply loved war for its own sake.
options.note: No such personal motive is given; the text attributes the hardening to the LORD's purpose.

options.text: Sihon feared losing his throne to a rival king.
options.note: No such political threat is mentioned in the text.

options.text: No reason is given for his refusal.
options.note: Verse 30 gives an explicit theological reason.

quiz.q: What happened to the cities of Sihon's kingdom after Israel defeated him (v.34)?

options.text: They were devoted to destruction, including women and children, with no survivors left.
options.note: Verse 34 states this plainly and starkly.

options.text: They were spared and resettled peacefully by Israel.
options.note: The text describes total destruction, not peaceful resettlement.

options.text: They surrendered without any fighting.
options.note: Verse 32 describes Sihon coming out for battle at Jahaz; there was real combat.

options.text: Israel avoided those cities entirely.
options.note: Verse 34 says Israel captured all of Sihon's cities.

quiz.q: How long did Israel's journey take from Kadesh-barnea until they crossed the Brook of Zered (v.14)?

options.text: Thirty-eight years, until that whole generation of fighting men had perished.
options.note: Verse 14 gives this exact figure and reason.

options.text: Eleven days.
options.note: Eleven days is the length of the direct route from Horeb to Kadesh-barnea mentioned in 1:2, not this later span.

options.text: Forty years exactly.
options.note: The total wilderness period is forty years, but this specific span from Kadesh-barnea to the Brook of Zered is thirty-eight.

options.text: Two years.
options.note: The text specifies thirty-eight years, not two.

questions: God set firm boundaries even for nations outside His covenant, giving Edom, Moab, and Ammon their own land. What does it teach you about God's character that His sovereign care extends even to people outside 'His people'?
Sihon's stubborn heart still served God's larger purpose, even in open opposition. Where might God be turning someone's resistance, or even your own past resistance, toward His purposes?

Israel had to wait thirty-eight years, watching a whole generation pass away, before this new chapter of victory could begin. Where are you in a season of waiting that feels wasted, and how might God still be at work in it?

Israel was told plainly not to provoke Edom, Moab, or Ammon and to accept the limits God had set. Where do you need to accept a limit God has placed rather than reaching for what is not yours to take?
Moses reminds Israel, 'you have lacked nothing' even in the wilderness. Where have you overlooked God's provision during a season that felt like aimless wandering?

translationNotes.note: These parenthetical historical notes would have been common knowledge to Moses' original audience; they explain that the displacement Israel is about to carry out against the Canaanites already has precedent in how Edom, Moab, and Ammon obtained their own lands.
The figure of thirty-eight years ties this account directly to the forty-year sentence pronounced in Numbers 14, marking this as the tail end of that period of judgment.

'Hardened his spirit' uses language that closely mirrors the hardening of Pharaoh's heart in Exodus, signaling a recurring biblical pattern of God's sovereign purpose working through real human stubbornness.
